answer:
Let $s$ be the number of steps the escalator has.
Let $v_u$ be the speed of the escalator when Petya is ascending, and $v_d$ be the speed of the escalator when Petya is descending.
Let $t_u$ be the time taken to ascend 75 steps, and $t_d$ be the time taken to descend 150 steps.
Let $v_w$ be the speed of Petya when ascending, and $v_w'$ be the speed of Petya when descending.
We are given that $v_w' = 3v_w$.

When ascending, Petya counts 75 steps. The distance covered is $75 \cdot \text{step size}$.
The speed of the escalator is $v_u$. The speed of Petya is $v_w$.
The relative speed of Petya with respect to the ground is $v_u + v_w$.
So, $t_u = \frac{75}{\text{step size}} / (v_u + v_w)$.
When descending, Petya counts 150 steps. The distance covered is $150 \cdot \text{step size}$.
The speed of the escalator is $v_d$. The speed of Petya is $v_w'$.
The relative speed of Petya with respect to the ground is $v_d - v_w'$.
So, $t_d = \frac{150}{\text{step size}} / (v_d - v_w')$.

We are given that $v_w' = 3v_w$.
We also know that the distance covered by the escalator in time $t$ is $v \cdot t$, where $v$ is the speed of the escalator.
When ascending, the escalator moves with speed $v_u$, and Petya moves with speed $v_w$.
The effective speed of Petya is $v_u + v_w$.
The time taken to ascend 75 steps is $t_u = \frac{75}{\text{step size}} / (v_u + v_w)$.
When descending, the escalator moves with speed $v_d$, and Petya moves with speed $3v_w$.
The effective speed of Petya is $v_d - 3v_w$.
The time taken to descend 150 steps is $t_d = \frac{150}{\text{step size}} / (v_d - 3v_w)$.

Since the escalator is moving, the distance covered by the escalator in time $t$ is $v t$.
Let $s$ be the number of steps the escalator has.
When ascending, the distance covered by the escalator is $v_u t_u = v_u \frac{75}{\text{step size}}$.
When descending, the distance covered by the escalator is $v_d t_d = v_d \frac{150}{\text{step size}}$.
Since the escalator is the same, the distance covered by the escalator is the same in both cases.
So, $v_u \frac{75}{\text{step size}} = v_d \frac{150}{\text{step size}}$.
$75 v_u = 150 v_d$, which implies $v_u = 2 v_d$.

We have $t_u = \frac{75}{\text{step size}} / (v_u + v_w)$ and $t_d = \frac{150}{\text{step size}} / (v_d - 3v_w)$.
Since the time taken to ascend is the same as the time taken to descend, we have $t_u = t_d$.
$\frac{75}{\text{step size}} / (v_u + v_w) = \frac{150}{\text{step size}} / (v_d - 3v_w)$
$\frac{75}{v_u + v_w} = \frac{150}{v_d - 3v_w}$
$75(v_d - 3v_w) = 150(v_u + v_w)$
$v_d - 3v_w = 2(v_u + v_w)$
$v_d - 3v_w = 2v_u + 2v_w$
$v_d = 2v_u + 5v_w$
Since $v_u = 2v_d$, we have $v_d = 2(2v_d) + 5v_w$, so $v_d = 4v_d + 5v_w$.
$-3v_d = 5v_w$, so $v_w = -\frac{3}{5} v_d$. This is not possible since $v_w$ must be positive.

Let $x$ be the step size.
The distance ascended is $75x$. The distance descended is $150x$.
Let $v_e$ be the speed of the escalator.
The time taken to ascend is $t_u = \frac{75x}{v_e}$.
The time taken to descend is $t_d = \frac{150x}{v_e}$.
The speed of Petya when ascending is $v_a$. The speed of Petya when descending is $3v_a$.
$v_e + v_a = \frac{75x}{t_u}$ and $v_e - 3v_a = \frac{150x}{t_d}$.
Since $t_u = \frac{75x}{v_e}$ and $t_d = \frac{150x}{v_e}$, we have $t_d = 2t_u$.
$v_e - 3v_a = \frac{150x}{t_d} = \frac{150x}{2t_u} = \frac{75x}{t_u} = v_e + v_a$.
$v_e - 3v_a = v_e + v_a$, so $4v_a = 0$, which implies $v_a = 0$. This is not possible.

Let $s$ be the number of steps the escalator has.
Let $v$ be the speed of the escalator.
Let $a$ be the speed of Petya when ascending, and $3a$ be the speed of Petya when descending.
The time taken to ascend 75 steps is $t_1 = \frac{75}{v+a}$.
The time taken to descend 150 steps is $t_2 = \frac{150}{v-3a}$.
Since the time taken is the same, $\frac{75}{v+a} = \frac{150}{v-3a}$.
$75(v-3a) = 150(v+a)$
$v-3a = 2(v+a)$
$v-3a = 2v+2a$
$v = -5a$. This is not possible since $v$ and $a$ must be positive.

Let $x$ be the step size.
Let $v$ be the speed of the escalator.
Let $t_u$ be the time taken to ascend 75 steps.
Let $t_d$ be the time taken to descend 150 steps.
The distance ascended is $75x$. The distance descended is $150x$.
The speed of Petya when ascending is $v_a$. The speed of Petya when descending is $3v_a$.
The speed of the escalator is $v$.
The effective speed when ascending is $v+v_a$. The effective speed when descending is $v-3v_a$.
$t_u = \frac{75x}{v+v_a}$ and $t_d = \frac{150x}{v-3v_a}$.
Since $t_u = t_d$, we have $\frac{75x}{v+v_a} = \frac{150x}{v-3v_a}$.
$\frac{75}{v+v_a} = \frac{150}{v-3v_a}$
$75(v-3v_a) = 150(v+v_a)$
$v-3v_a = 2(v+v_a)$
$v-3v_a = 2v+2v_a$
$v = -5v_a$. This is impossible.
